topics.title: Strings
topics.body:
JSON strings are sequences of Unicode characters enclosed in **double quotes** (`"`). Single quotes are not valid JSON. Use backslash escapes for special characters: `\"` (quote), `\\` (backslash), `\/` (forward slash), `\n` (newline), `\r` (carriage return), `\t` (tab), `\b` (backspace), `\f` (form feed), and `\uXXXX` (Unicode code point).

JSON strings are always UTF-8 encoded when written to files.

topics.title: Numbers
topics.body:
JSON supports **integers** and **floating-point numbers**. There is no separate integer type in the spec — all numbers are IEEE 754 double-precision floats in most implementations.

Valid forms: `42`, `-7`, `3.14`, `2.5e10`, `-1.2E-3`. Not valid: `0x1F` (hex), `NaN`, `Infinity`, trailing commas in number literals, or leading zeros (`01`).

topics.title: Booleans
topics.body:
JSON booleans are the lowercase literals `true` and `false` — no quotes, no capitalisation. They map to `Bool` in Swift, `boolean` in JavaScript, and similar in other languages.

Common mistake: using `"true"` (a string) instead of `true` (the boolean literal).

topics.title: Null
topics.body:
`null` is the JSON representation of an absent or unknown value. It is the lowercase literal `null` — not `NULL`, `Null`, `None`, or `nil`.

In Swift, a JSON `null` decodes to `nil` on an optional property. In JavaScript it is distinct from `undefined`.

topics.title: Arrays
topics.body:
JSON arrays are **ordered lists** of values enclosed in square brackets `[…]`, with elements separated by commas. Elements can be any JSON type and do not need to be the same type.

Arrays are zero-indexed when accessed in most languages. Trailing commas after the last element are **not** valid JSON.

topics.title: Objects
topics.body:
JSON objects are **unordered** collections of key–value pairs enclosed in curly braces `{…}`. Keys must be **strings** (double-quoted). Values can be any JSON type.

Trailing commas after the last pair are **not** valid JSON. Duplicate keys are technically allowed by the spec but have implementation-defined behaviour — avoid them.

topics.title: Key–Value Pairs
topics.body:
Each entry in a JSON object is a **key–value pair**: a string key, a colon `:`, and a value. Keys must be unique within a single object (duplicates are undefined behaviour).

Whitespace around the colon and between pairs is allowed and ignored. Elements are separated by commas — no trailing comma after the last pair.

topics.title: Object Syntax
topics.body:
An object begins with `{` and ends with `}`. Inside, each key–value pair is separated by a comma. **No trailing comma** is allowed after the last pair.

Objects can be empty: `{}`. They can contain other objects, arrays, or any mix of JSON types.

topics.title: Array Syntax
topics.body:
An array begins with `[` and ends with `]`. Elements are separated by commas. **No trailing comma** is allowed after the last element.

Arrays can be empty: `[]`. They can contain heterogeneous types (strings, numbers, objects, arrays, etc.).

topics.title: Nested Structures
topics.body:
JSON supports arbitrary nesting of objects and arrays. An object value can itself be an object or an array, and vice versa. Depth is theoretically unlimited but parsers often impose a practical limit (e.g. 512 levels).

Keep nesting depth reasonable for readability — deeply nested structures are harder to diff and debug.

topics.title: Optional Fields
topics.body:
JSON has no built-in concept of optional fields. Two common conventions:
1. **Omit the key** when the value is absent — the consumer must handle a missing key.
2. **Set the value to `null`** — the key is always present, value signals absence.

Be consistent within an API. Swift's `Codable` treats missing keys and `null` differently: a missing key throws unless the property is optional; `null` maps to `nil`.

topics.title: JSON Schema
topics.body:
**JSON Schema** is a vocabulary for annotating and validating JSON documents. A schema describes the expected shape — required fields, types, string patterns, number ranges, array constraints, etc.

Schemas are themselves JSON objects (draft 2020-12 or earlier drafts). Tools like `ajv` (JavaScript) or `JSONSchemaDraft4` (Swift) can validate documents against a schema at runtime.

topics.title: Validation
topics.body:
JSON validation checks that a document is **syntactically correct** (well-formed JSON) and optionally **semantically correct** (matches a schema).

Sputnik's JSON editor validates syntax on every keystroke using `JSONSerialization` and shows errors in the gutter. For schema validation, use a dedicated validator tool or library.

topics.title: Pretty-Print & Minify
topics.body:
**Pretty-printing** adds indentation and newlines to make JSON human-readable. **Minifying** removes all non-essential whitespace to reduce file size.

In Swift: `JSONSerialization.data(withJSONObject:options: .prettyPrinted)` produces pretty output. Without the option, the output is minified.

Sputnik's JSON Viewer (Module 8) provides a Prettify / Minify toggle.

topics.title: Parsing & Serialisation
topics.body:
**Parsing** converts a JSON string into an in-memory data structure. **Serialising** (or stringifying) converts a data structure back to a JSON string.

In Swift, use `JSONDecoder` / `JSONEncoder` with `Codable` types for type-safe parsing, or `JSONSerialization` for dynamic `[String: Any]` access.

topics.title: JSON5
topics.body:
**JSON5** is a superset of JSON that allows:
- Single-line (`//`) and block (`/* */`) comments
- Trailing commas in objects and arrays
- Single-quoted strings
- Unquoted object keys (if valid identifiers)
- Hexadecimal numbers (`0xCAFE`)
- Multi-line strings

JSON5 is **not** valid JSON. Most parsers do not support it. Config files (`.json5`) often use this format. Do not confuse with standard JSON.

topics.title: JSONC (JSON with Comments)
topics.body:
**JSONC** is JSON extended with C-style comments (`//` and `/* */`). It is used by VS Code settings files and some configuration tools.

Like JSON5, JSONC is **not** standard JSON. A JSONC parser strips comments before parsing the rest as JSON. Sputnik's JSON editor validates standard JSON only.

topics.title: JSON-LD
topics.body:
**JSON-LD** (JSON for Linked Data) is a W3C standard for representing semantic/linked data as JSON. It uses a `@context` key to map JSON properties to IRIs (Internationalized Resource Identifiers).

JSON-LD documents are valid JSON, but they carry additional semantic meaning. Used in SEO structured data, knowledge graphs, and the Semantic Web.

topics.title: GeoJSON
topics.body:
**GeoJSON** (RFC 7946) is a JSON format for encoding geographic data — points, lines, polygons, and collections. The root object has a `type` key.

Common types: `Point`, `LineString`, `Polygon`, `MultiPoint`, `MultiLineString`, `MultiPolygon`, `GeometryCollection`, `Feature`, `FeatureCollection`.

topics.title: Common JSON Errors
topics.body:
The most frequent JSON syntax errors:

1. **Trailing comma** — `{"a": 1,}` — not allowed after the last element.
2. **Single quotes** — `{'key': 'value'}` — only double quotes are valid.
3. **Unquoted key** — `{key: "value"}` — all keys must be quoted strings.
4. **Comments** — `// comment` — standard JSON has no comments.
5. **NaN / Infinity** — not valid JSON number literals; use `null` instead.
6. **Hex numbers** — `0xFF` — not valid; only decimal integers and floats.
7. **Trailing decimal point** — `1.` — not valid; write `1.0`.

topics.title: Best Practices
topics.body:
Guidelines for writing clean, portable JSON:

- **Use consistent key casing** — `camelCase` is conventional in JavaScript APIs; `snake_case` in Python/Ruby. Pick one and stick to it.
- **Keep arrays homogeneous** — mixing types in one array makes parsing fragile.
- **Prefer explicit nulls over missing keys** when absence is meaningful.
- **Avoid deep nesting** — flatter structures are easier to query and cache.
- **Use ISO 8601 strings for dates** — `"2024-01-15T09:30:00Z"` — JSON has no native date type.
- **Limit numeric precision** — not all parsers handle all doubles identically. Safe integer range: `-(2^53 - 1)` to `2^53 - 1`.

topics.title: Encoding & Unicode
topics.body:
JSON files **must** be encoded in **UTF-8**, UTF-16, or UTF-32. UTF-8 without BOM is strongly recommended (RFC 8259). In practice, nearly all JSON tooling assumes UTF-8.

Any Unicode character can appear in a string either literally (if valid UTF-8) or as a `\uXXXX` escape. Characters above U+FFFF require surrogate pairs: `\uD800\uDC00` for U+10000.

Non-ASCII characters in keys are valid but may cause interoperability problems with some parsers.

topics.title: Size & Performance
topics.body:
Techniques for keeping JSON payloads lean:

- **Minify** for production — strip whitespace.
- **Shorten key names** in high-volume APIs (`"id"` not `"identifier"`).
- **Use arrays of arrays** instead of arrays of objects when keys are shared and the schema is fixed.
- **Compress with gzip/brotli** — JSON compresses well due to repeated keys and structure.
- **Pagination** large datasets instead of returning everything at once.
- **NDJSON** (Newline-Delimited JSON) for streaming large datasets: one JSON value per line.

topics.title: JSON vs Other Formats
topics.body:
**JSON vs XML**: JSON is more compact and easier to parse in JavaScript. XML supports namespaces, DTDs, and mixed content; JSON does not.

**JSON vs YAML**: YAML is a superset of JSON (most valid JSON is valid YAML 1.2). YAML allows comments and more data types but is harder to parse and whitespace-sensitive.

**JSON vs TOML**: TOML is designed for config files; more readable for flat or shallow data. JSON is better for deeply nested data or API responses.

**JSON vs MessagePack/CBOR**: Binary formats; much more compact but not human-readable.

topics.title: Dates & Times
topics.body:
JSON has **no native date type**. The standard approach is to encode dates as **ISO 8601 strings**: `"2024-01-15"` (date only) or `"2024-01-15T09:30:00Z"` (UTC datetime) or `"2024-01-15T09:30:00+01:00"` (with offset).

Alternatives: Unix timestamps (integers) for compact storage, but they are less readable. Always document which format your API uses.

Swift's `JSONDecoder` supports `.iso8601` date decoding strategy out of the box.
